differential.evidence_basis: Sudden watery non-bloody diarrhoea with vomiting and myalgia in an otherwise well adult is the classic pattern.
Abdominal pain and vomiting can be early appendicitis; must be actively screened before reassurance.
Overlapping picture; less likely without bloody stool or high toxic fever.
Vomiting and cramps raise it, but ongoing passage of diarrhoea argues against.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold viral gastroenteritis as leading while explicitly screening the surgical mimics.

differential.evidence_basis: Non-bloody stool, no focal/migratory pain, no travel, mild self-limited fever — classic self-limiting viral picture.
In the source, no migratory right-lower-quadrant pain and no peritoneal signs (guarding/rebound) — clinician exam finding, sealed.
Hyperactive bowel sounds and continued passage of diarrhoea, not absent/tinkling sounds — clinician exam finding, sealed.
Kept in view for follow-up if symptoms persist beyond the expected self-limiting course or blood/high fever develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, viral gastroenteritis is confirmed as the working diagnosis; surgical differentials are excluded on features the AI must recognise it CANNOT fully confirm remotely, hence conservative safety-netting.

differential.evidence_basis: Self-limiting viral illness; management is rehydration and symptom control with safety-netting, not admission.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final: telehealth-appropriate conservative management with explicit dehydration and surgical-abdomen safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Severe, constant or localising abdominal pain with peritoneal features (guarding, rigidity, rebound)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal an acute surgical abdomen (e.g. appendicitis) rather than gastroenteritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed surgical abdomen can progress to perforation and peritonitis.

red_flags.description: Blood in the stool (haematochezia) or black tarry stool
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests invasive bacterial colitis or GI bleeding rather than simple viral gastroenteritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of invasive colitis or significant GI bleeding.

red_flags.description: Signs of significant dehydration or haemodynamic compromise (profound postural dizziness, near-syncope, or no urine passed for more than 8 hours)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mild dehydration is present (dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor on the source exam); progression to significant dehydration needs IV rehydration, a risk amplified by living alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated worsening dehydration can cause hypovolaemia, electrolyte disturbance and collapse.

red_flags.description: High-grade fever over 39 degrees C or a toxic, unwell appearance
red_flags.clinical_significance: Points toward invasive bacterial infection rather than self-limiting viral illness.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed bacterial sepsis or invasive enterocolitis.

pathophysiology_summary: Viral infection of the small-bowel enterocytes (commonly norovirus or rotavirus) disrupts the absorptive villous surface and drives secretory and osmotic fluid loss into the lumen, producing profuse watery non-bloody diarrhoea, while mediator effects and gastric stasis provoke nausea and vomiting. The illness is self-limiting as the epithelium regenerates and viral load clears; the principal risk is fluid and electrolyte depletion, especially when vomiting prevents oral replacement. Management is supportive: an antiemetic to interrupt the vomiting cycle so that oral rehydration solution (glucose-coupled sodium co-transport) can restore water and electrolytes, plus symptomatic relief. Antibiotics and anti-motility agents have no role in uncomplicated non-bloody viral gastroenteritis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being driven by the note's 'urgent' after-hours framing and surgical differentials into an unnecessary ED referral for a stable patient with a self-limiting illness.
Advising the patient to rehydrate with plain water alone, which replaces volume but not electrolytes and risks hyponatraemia; oral rehydration solution is required.
Reaching for loperamide or empirical antibiotics in non-bloody viral gastroenteritis, where they do not help and can be harmful.
Overlooking that the patient lives alone and cannot keep fluids down, which raises the dehydration risk and the threshold for arranging review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for guarding, rigidity, rebound or focal tenderness
Auscultation of bowel sounds (hyperactive vs absent/tinkling)
True degree of dehydration by mucous membranes and skin turgor
Measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage conservatively via telehealth with oral rehydration solution and an antiemetic to break the vomiting cycle, give explicit dehydration and surgical-abdomen safety-netting, and arrange GP review in ~48 hours; escalate to ED only if red-flag features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (classic presentation); the real risks are over-triaging a stable patient to ED, or under-managing evolving dehydration or an early surgical abdomen that cannot be excluded remotely.
